title: Homeowners Insurance
description: Distinguish homeowners policy forms, understand coverage sections, and apply coinsurance concepts

sections.title: Homeowners Policy Forms
items: HO-1: Basic Form - Named perils only (10), rarely offered today
HO-2: Broad Form - Named perils (16), moderate protection
HO-3: Special Form (Most Common) - Dwelling: Open perils (all-risk), Personal property: Named perils
HO-4: Renters Insurance - No dwelling coverage, personal property named perils, liability included
HO-5: Comprehensive Form - Both dwelling and personal property: Open perils (broadest)
HO-6: Condo Unit Owners - Walls-in coverage, HO association covers building exterior
HO-8: Modified Coverage Form - For older homes, pays actual cash value (not replacement)

sections.title: Section I: Property Coverage
items: Coverage A - Dwelling: House structure, attached structures, replacement cost basis
Coverage B - Other Structures: Detached garage, shed, fence; 10% of Coverage A; not used for business
Coverage C - Personal Property: Belongings inside and away from home; 50-70% of Coverage A; worldwide coverage (some limits)
Coverage D - Loss of Use: Additional living expenses, fair rental value; 20-30% of Coverage A

sections.title: Valuable Items Solution
sections.content: Schedule valuable items with specific coverage to overcome sublimits. A scheduled personal property endorsement provides agreed-value coverage for jewelry, art, collectibles, etc.

sections.title: Section II: Liability Coverage
items: Coverage E - Personal Liability: Bodily injury/property damage claims, legal defense costs; Minimum $100,000 (recommend $300,000+)
Coverage F - Medical Payments to Others: No-fault payment for guest injuries; $1,000-$5,000 typical; NOT for household members

sections.title: Valuation Methods
items: Replacement Cost: Cost to replace with like kind and quality; no deduction for depreciation; standard for dwelling
Actual Cash Value (ACV): Replacement Cost - Depreciation; example: 10-year roof ($15,000) with 50% depreciation pays $7,500
Functional Replacement Cost: Replace with functionally similar but not identical; modern materials for older home features; used in HO-8

sections.title: Coinsurance (80% Rule)
sections.content: Coinsurance requires insuring dwelling at minimum 80% of replacement cost. Penalty formula: Payment = (Coverage Carried ÷ Coverage Required) × Loss.

sections.title: Guaranteed Replacement Cost
sections.content: Guaranteed replacement cost eliminates coinsurance concerns. It pays full rebuild even if over limit. Available for additional premium.

sections.title: Common Exclusions
items: Section I Exclusions: Flood, earthquake, war, nuclear hazard, neglect/maintenance, ordinance or law (unless endorsed), mold (usually limited)
Section II Exclusions: Business activities, professional liability, motor vehicles (on public roads), watercraft (over certain size), intentional acts, workers compensation situations

sections.title: Exam Tip: DUPLE Mnemonic
sections.content: HO Coverage Sections: Dwelling, Use (loss of), Personal property, Liability, Everyone's medical (Coverage F).

sections.title: Summary
items: HO-3 is most common: open perils on dwelling, named perils on personal property
Coverage B (other structures) = 10% of A; Coverage C (personal property) = 50-70% of A
80% coinsurance requires insuring to 80% of replacement cost to avoid penalties
Flood and earthquake require separate policies
Personal liability coverage applies to negligence claims anywhere

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following homeowners insurance policy forms is designed for condominium owners?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: HO-6 is specifically designed for condominium owners. It covers improvements and betterments to the unit, personal property, and liability coverage. HO-2, HO-3, and HO-8 are designed for other types of dwellings.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which type of dwelling each policy is meant to cover.

reveal.question: Explain the purpose and consequences of the coinsurance clause in a homeowners insurance policy.
reveal.answer: The coinsurance clause requires the homeowner to insure the dwelling for a specified percentage (usually 80%) of its replacement cost. If the homeowner fails to meet this requirement, any covered loss will be paid proportionally, based on the actual amount of insurance carried compared to the amount that should have been carried. This encourages homeowners to adequately insure their property.

knowledgeCheck.question: John's home is insured under an HO-3 policy with a Coverage A (Dwelling) limit of $300,000. A fire causes $200,000 in damage. It is determined that the full replacement cost of the home is $400,000. Assuming an 80% coinsurance requirement, how much will the insurance company pay, before any applicable deductible?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the required coverage: $400,000 (replacement cost) * 80% (coinsurance) = $320,000. Since John only has $300,000 in coverage, he has not met the coinsurance requirement. The payout is calculated as follows: ($300,000 (actual coverage) / $320,000 (required coverage)) * $200,000 (loss) = $187,500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the coinsurance formula: (Amount of Insurance Carried / Amount of Insurance Required) * Loss = Insurance Payment

sections.title: Actual Cash Value vs. Replacement Cost

rows.label: Definition
values: The cost to replace property with new property of like kind and quality, without deduction for depreciation.

rows.label: Claim Payment
values: Claim payment will be lower, as depreciation is deducted.
Claim payment will cover the full cost of replacement (up to policy limits), without deduction for depreciation.

title: Auto Insurance
description: Explain PAP coverage parts, apply liability coverage concepts, and understand UM/UIM coverage

sections.title: Personal Auto Policy (PAP) Structure
sections.content: PAP has 6 parts: Liability, Medical, Uninsured, Physical damage, Duties (and provisions). Remember: PAP LMUPD.

sections.title: Part A: Liability Coverage
sections.content: Pays for bodily injury and property damage to others when you're at fault. Legal defense costs are in addition to limits. Covers volunteer workers using your car.

sections.title: Split Limits vs. Combined Single Limit
items: Split Limits: Expressed as three numbers (e.g., 100/300/50): $100,000 per person BI, $300,000 per accident BI, $50,000 property damage
Combined Single Limit (CSL): One limit for all coverages (e.g., $500,000 CSL for any combination of BI/PD)

sections.title: Part B: Medical Payments (MedPay)
items: Pays medical expenses for you and passengers
Regardless of fault
Typical limits: $5,000-$10,000
Per person limit

sections.title: Part C: Uninsured/Underinsured Motorist
items: Uninsured Motorist (UM): Covers you when hit by driver with no insurance, hit-and-run, or insurer insolvent
Underinsured Motorist (UIM): Covers when at-fault driver has insufficient coverage; your UIM covers the difference

sections.title: Part D: Physical Damage
items: Collision: Damage from collision with object or vehicle; regardless of fault; subject to deductible
Comprehensive (Other Than Collision): Theft, vandalism, weather (hail, flood, wind), fire, hitting an animal, glass breakage

sections.title: Who and What is Insured
items: Who: Named insured and family members, anyone using covered vehicle with permission
Vehicles: Listed vehicles, newly acquired vehicles (coverage period varies), temporary substitute vehicles, non-owned vehicles used by insured

sections.title: Liability Exclusions
items: Intentional injury
Vehicles with fewer than 4 wheels
Vehicles used as public transportation
Business use of vehicle (other than private passenger)

sections.title: No-Fault Insurance
sections.content: Pure no-fault (rare) means you cannot sue for pain/suffering and your insurer pays regardless of fault. Modified no-fault (most states) requires Personal Injury Protection (PIP) but allows suit if injuries exceed threshold. PIP covers medical expenses, lost wages, essential services, and funeral expenses.

sections.title: Summary
items: PAP has 6 parts: Liability, Med Pay, UM/UIM, Physical Damage, Duties, General
Split limits: Per person BI / Per accident BI / Property damage
Collision covers impact with objects; Comprehensive covers all other perils
UM covers when at-fault driver has no insurance; UIM covers insufficient coverage
Legal defense costs are in addition to liability limits

knowledgeCheck.question:
Sarah, while volunteering for a local charity, is using her personal vehicle to deliver meals to seniors. She causes an accident, injuring another driver and damaging their car. Sarah's auto insurance policy has liability limits of 100/300/50. Which of the following is TRUE regarding Sarah's coverage?

I. Sarah's liability coverage will cover the bodily injury and property damage to the other driver, up to the policy limits.
II. Sarah's legal defense costs associated with the accident are included within the 100/300/50 limits.
III. Sarah's auto insurance policy covers volunteer work using her car.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: I is true because the liability coverage covers bodily injury and property damage when Sarah is at fault, up to the policy limits. III is true because the lesson states that liability coverage covers volunteer workers using your car. II is incorrect because legal defense costs are typically paid in addition to the stated policy limits.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember how liability coverage works for bodily injury, property damage, and legal defense. Also, recall if volunteer work is covered.

rows.label: Covered Peril
values: Impact with another vehicle or object, regardless of fault.
Damage from almost any other peril, such as theft, vandalism, fire, hail, or hitting an animal.

rows.label: Example Scenario
values: Hitting a tree after losing control of your vehicle.
Windshield cracked by a falling tree branch.

rows.label: Fault Matters?
values: No, collision coverage applies regardless of fault.
No, comprehensive coverage applies regardless of fault.

reveal.question: What does Uninsured/Underinsured Motorist coverage protect you from?
reveal.answer: It protects you if you are injured by a driver who either has no insurance (uninsured) or has insufficient insurance to cover your damages (underinsured). It covers your bodily injuries, and in some states, property damage.

title: Umbrella Liability and Other Coverage
description: Explain umbrella policy purpose and structure, and understand professional liability coverage
topics: Explain umbrella policy purpose and structure
Identify coverage gaps filled by umbrella

sections.title: Personal Umbrella Policy Purpose
sections.content: Umbrella policies provide additional liability limits AND fill gaps in underlying coverage. Typical limits range from $1,000,000 to $5,000,000+ for the whole family.

sections.title: How Umbrella Works
items: Underlying policies exhausted first
Umbrella covers excess (up to limit)
Some claims covered only by umbrella (with SIR)

sections.title: Self-Insured Retention (SIR)
sections.content: For claims covered by umbrella but NOT underlying, the SIR acts as a deductible (typically $250-$10,000). Examples include libel, slander, and invasion of privacy claims.

sections.title: Without Umbrella
sections.content: Without umbrella, you would pay $1,200,000 out of pocket. Your home, income, and assets would all be at risk.

sections.title: What Umbrella Covers
items: Bodily injury liability
Property damage liability
Personal injury (libel, slander, defamation)
Broader geographic coverage
Legal defense costs

sections.title: Umbrella Exclusions
items: Business activities
Professional liability
Workers compensation
Intentional acts
Contractual liability (some)

sections.title: Who Needs Umbrella?
items: Anyone with assets to protect
Visible career/public profile
Teen drivers
Swimming pool owners
Rental property owners
Pet owners (especially certain breeds)
Frequent entertainers

sections.title: Professional Liability Insurance
items: Errors and Omissions (E&O): For professional services - financial advisors, insurance agents, consultants, real estate agents
Malpractice Insurance: For licensed professionals - doctors, lawyers, accountants, architects/engineers
Directors and Officers (D&O): For corporate boards/executives - wrongful act claims, shareholder suits, regulatory actions

sections.title: Tail Coverage
sections.content: Tail coverage provides an extended reporting period, critical when leaving practice. It covers claims filed after the policy ends for incidents that occurred during the policy period.

sections.title: Other Important Coverages
items: Flood Insurance: Not covered by homeowners; NFIP; required in designated flood zones; max $250,000 dwelling / $100,000 contents
Earthquake Insurance: Not covered by homeowners; high deductibles (10-25% of value); critical in seismic zones
Identity Theft Coverage: Credit monitoring, recovery expenses, legal fees, lost wages
Special Events Coverage: Weddings, parties, liability for event

sections.title: Annual Review Checklist
items: Update home value (inflation)
Review vehicle list
Assess liability exposure
Check special items (jewelry, art)
Verify umbrella requirements met
Update beneficiaries (life insurance)

sections.title: Exam Tip: SAFE Mnemonic
sections.content: Separate Policies Needed: Specialty equipment, Auto (for vehicle damage), Flood, Earthquake.

sections.title: Summary
items: Umbrella provides excess liability AND fills coverage gaps
Self-insured retention applies when umbrella covers what underlying does not
Underlying policy minimums must be met for umbrella eligibility
Professional liability uses claims-made—tail coverage critical when leaving
Flood and earthquake require separate policies beyond homeowners

sections.title: Umbrella Policy and SIR Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: John has an umbrella policy with a $1,000,000 limit and a $5,000 Self-Insured Retention (SIR). He is sued for libel, and the court awards damages of $25,000. His underlying homeowner's policy does NOT cover libel. How much will John have to pay out-of-pocket?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since the underlying policy doesn't cover libel, the umbrella policy will cover the claim, but John must first pay the $5,000 SIR. The umbrella will then cover the remaining $20,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider whether the underlying policy covers the claim when dealing with the SIR.

reveal.question: What is a Self-Insured Retention (SIR) in the context of an umbrella liability policy?
reveal.answer: A Self-Insured Retention (SIR) is the amount of money the insured must pay out-of-pocket before the umbrella policy will cover a claim if the underlying insurance policy does not cover the loss.

knowledgeCheck.question: Susan is a financial planner. A client sues her for making a negligent recommendation that resulted in a significant financial loss. Which type of insurance policy would MOST likely cover this claim?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions) insurance specifically covers claims arising from professional negligence or errors in the services provided. Umbrella policies typically cover personal liability, not professional malpractice.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the type of claim being made - is it personal or professional?
